general: The first interrupt controllers date to the 1950s UNIVAC — this bunker is older logic than you think.
UNIX time will roll over its 32-bit signed counter in 2038 — the original Y2K, replayed.
The term "bug" predates software: Grace Hopper's team logged an actual moth jamming a relay in 1947.
Early mainframes were programmed by physically rewiring plugboards — no keyboard, just cables.
The banker's algorithm for deadlock avoidance is named for the exact problem it solves: never promise more credit than you can cover.
Some early OSes ran with no memory protection at all — one crashing program could take down everything.
Round robin scheduling was popularized by time-sharing systems in the 1960s, when one computer had to feel instant to dozens of people at once.
The first disk drive, IBM's RAMAC, held about 5MB across fifty spinning platters the size of dinner plates.

1.1: The system bus concept dates back to the PDP-8 in 1965 — one shared highway, every component takes a turn.
1.2: Interrupts exist because polling every device in a loop would waste most of a CPU's cycles waiting on nothing.
